1: Adonizedek ('my lord is righteous') king of Jerusalem responds to Gibeon's treaty. kî lāqaḥ yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-hāgibʿôn wayyaḥărîmehā — the king conflates Jericho's destruction with Gibeon's capitulation; from Jerusalem's perspective, both represent existential threats. The name Adonizedek echoes Melchizedek ('king of righteousness') from Genesis 14:18 — a Jerusalem king whose name invokes righteousness now stands opposed to Israel.
2: Gibeon is described as 'like one of the royal cities, greater than Ai' — geographic and strategic context. hî ʿîr gĕdôlāh kĕʾaḥat hāʿārîm hammamlākāh: Gibeon's size and political status made its defection to Israel a seismic event. All its fighting men (kol-ʾănāšêhā gibbōrîm) had been Israel's potential adversaries; now they are treaty partners. Jerusalem's alarm is geopolitical, not merely religious.
3: The coalition of five Amorite kings — Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon — represents a major southern military alliance. These cities controlled the Shephelah access routes; their cooperation signals coordinated Canaanite resistance to what they recognize as an existential threat. Adonizedek's role as initiator establishes Jerusalem as the political center of the resistance.
4: ʿălû ʾēlay wĕʿizerûnî wĕnakkeh ʾet-gibʿôn — 'come up to me and help me, and let us strike Gibeon.' The military verb nākāh (strike down) is the same used of YHWH striking the Egyptians; punishing Gibeon is the coalition's immediate goal, but the deeper target is reversing the breach in the Canaanite defensive line.
5: The five kings encamp against Gibeon. The siege is the direct cause of the all-night march in Joshua 10:9; Gibeon's treaty with Israel now triggers Israel's military obligation. YHWH's sovereignty is working through the coalition's aggression: by attacking Gibeon, they bring Israel's army to their doorstep.
6: Gibeon's appeal to Joshua is grounded in the treaty: ʾal-terep yādêkā meʿăbādêkā — 'do not relax your hand from your servants.' The phrase 'your servants' (ʾăbādîm) is the covenant-treaty language of Joshua 9; they invoke their status to claim Israel's military protection. The appeal is legally correct: the treaty obligates Israel to defend Gibeon.
7: wayyaʿal yĕhôšuaʿ min-haggillgāl hûʾ wĕkol-ʿam hammiḥāmāh ʿimmô — 'Joshua went up from Gilgal, he and all the people of war with him.' The entire fighting force moves out; this is not a relief party but a full military campaign. The rapid response signals Joshua's seriousness about treaty obligations — he does not hesitate despite the deception that created the obligation.
8: YHWH's direct word of assurance: ʾal-tîrāʾ mēhem kî bĕyādĕkā nĕtattîm — 'do not fear them, for into your hand I have given them.' The perfect tense is prophetic: the victory is treated as already accomplished. This formula ('do not fear; I have given them') is the standard divine war oracle that inaugurates YHWH's battles (Deuteronomy 3:2; Joshua 6:2; Judges 4:7).
9: wayyābōʾ ʾălêhem yĕhôšuaʿ pitʾōm kol-hallaylāh ʿālāh min-haggillgāl — 'Joshua came upon them suddenly, having gone up all night from Gilgal.' The tactical detail is remarkable: a forced night march (~35 km, 1,000m elevation gain) to achieve surprise. pitʾōm ('suddenly') is the key tactical word; surprise was the advantage Israel's smaller force needed. The speed echoes YHWH's mode of operation: he comes suddenly, when not expected.
10: wayhummēm YHWH lifnê yiśrāʾēl — 'YHWH threw them into confusion before Israel.' Hûm/hāmam is divine-panic vocabulary: the same verb YHWH used at the Red Sea (Exodus 14:24) and will use in later battles (1 Samuel 7:10). The 'great slaughter' at Gibeon gives way to pursuit; YHWH's confusion rout precedes human sword-work — the victory is his before it is Israel's.
11: wayyaṣṣēl YHWH ʾăbānîm gĕdōlôt min-haššāmayim ʿălêhem — 'YHWH rained large stones from heaven upon them.' The hailstones are described as lethal divine artillery; wayyāmutû rabbîm mēhem bĕʾabnê habbārād mēʾašer hēmîtû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl leḥāreb — 'more died from the hailstones than Israel killed with the sword.' The divine contribution exceeds the human. The same combination — human army plus divine bombardment — recurs in the eschatological battles of Revelation (Revelation 16:21: 100-lb hailstones).
12: ʾāz yĕdabbēr yĕhôšuaʿ lYHWH — 'then Joshua spoke to YHWH.' What follows (Joshua 10:12b-13) is a poetic quotation, set apart from the narrative. The address is to the sun and moon directly: šemeš bĕgibʿôn dôm wĕyārēaḥ bĕʿēmeq ʾayyālôn — 'Sun, in Gibeon, be silent/still; and moon, in the Valley of Aijalon.' The verb dôm (be still, be silent) is used of hushed awe before God (Psalms 37:7; Habakkuk 2:20); it may carry a sense of the cosmic realm falling silent before YHWH's battle-action, not merely of mechanical pause.
13: wayyiddōm haššemeš wĕyārēaḥ ʿāmād — 'the sun stood still and the moon stopped.' The Hebrew verbs dāmam (be still) and ʿāmad (stood, halted) both describe cessation of motion. The duration: kĕyôm tāmîm — 'like a complete/perfect day.' The Book of Jashar (sēper hayyāšār, 'Book of the Upright') is cited as the source, a now-lost anthology also referenced in 2 Samuel 1:18. The narrator invokes an external corroborating source for the most extraordinary claim in the chapter.
14: wĕlōʾ hāyāh kayyôm hahûʾ lĕpānāyw wĕʾaḥărāyw lišmōaʿ YHWH bĕqôl ʾîš — 'there was no day like it before or since, in YHWH listening to the voice of a man.' The superlative formula ('never before or since') marks a unique event. The theological point: YHWH listened to a human voice and acted cosmically. kî YHWH nilḥam lĕyiśrāʾēl — 'for YHWH fought for Israel' — the divine warrior formula; this is YHWH's battle before it is Joshua's.
15: wayyāšob yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkol-yiśrāʾēl ʿimmô ʾel-hammaḥăneh haggillgālāh — 'Joshua returned, all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.' Text critics note this verse seems out of place — the five kings are still hiding (Joshua 10:16-17) and the southern campaign continues through Joshua 10:43. Some manuscripts (LXX) omit it here. The verse may be a dislocation or an editorial notice marking the end of the poetic insert.
16: The five kings flee and hide in a cave at Makkedah. wayyimāsʾû nistarim bammĕʿārāh bĕmakkēdāh — hiding in a cave mirrors the imagery of enemies cowering before YHWH's advance (Isaiah 2:19: 'men will enter caves... before the terror of YHWH'). Their capture and execution will become a teaching moment for Israel's commanders.
17: The cave is found and reported to Joshua. The rapid intelligence (wayyuggad lĕyĕhôšuaʿ lēʾmōr) and Joshua's immediate response demonstrate coordinated campaign management; the main pursuit continues while the cave is secured — priorities correctly ordered.
18: gōllû ʾăbānîm gĕdōlôt ʾel-pî hamĕʿārāh — 'roll large stones to the mouth of the cave.' The stones serve dual purpose: prevent escape and mark the spot. The language of rolling stones into the mouth of a cave — sealing royalty inside — echoes in reverse in Gospel resurrection narratives (Mark 16:3; Matthew 27:60).
19: wĕʾattem ʾal-taʿamĕdû raddĕpû ʾaḥărê ʾōyĕbêkem — 'you, do not stand still; pursue after your enemies.' The imperative rādap (pursue) is urgent; half-measures in battle allow regroupment. The theological principle is embedded in the tactical instruction: YHWH has given them; do not leave the work half-done.
20: The slaughter is described as ʿad-tummām mĕʾōd — 'very great, until they were finished/consumed.' The remnant who survived reached their fortified cities. The two outcomes (total annihilation of those caught in the open; survival of those who reached fortified cities) explain why the south still required the mopping-up campaign of Joshua 10:28-43.
21: wayāšubû kol-hāʿām ʾel-hammaḥăneh ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ bĕmakkēdāh bĕšālôm — 'all the people returned safely to Joshua in the camp at Makkedah.' Bĕšālôm ('in peace/wholeness') marks the tactical return; no Israelite casualty is mentioned. lōʾ ḥāraṣ lĕbĕnê yiśrāʾēl lĕʾîš ʾet-lĕšōnô — 'not a man sharpened his tongue against Israel' — an idiom for hostile complaint or threat; the enemies are silenced.
22: Joshua commands the kings brought out. pĕtĕḥû ʾet-pî hamĕʿārāh wĕhōṣîʾû ʾēlay ʾet-ḥamēšet hamĕlākîm — 'open the mouth of the cave and bring out to me the five kings.' The verb pāttaḥ ('open the mouth') reverses the sealing of Joshua 10:18; this is deliberate ceremony, not mere practicality.
23: The five kings are named again — Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon. The geographic spread (north to south, hill country to Shephelah) encapsulates the southern region. Their naming here prepares for the teaching moment in Joshua 10:24-25 and the subsequent campaign that conquers their cities individually (Joshua 10:28-39).
24: wayyiqrāʾ yĕhôšuaʿ ʾel-kol-ʾîš yiśrāʾēl wayyōʾmer ʾel-qĕṣînê ʾanšê hammiḥāmāh — 'Joshua called all the men of Israel and said to the commanders.' The ritual of placing feet on the necks of defeated kings (śîmû ʾet-raglêkem ʿal-ṣawwĕrê hammĕlākîm) is documented in ANE victory iconography; Egyptian pharaohs and Assyrian kings are shown with feet on prostrate enemies. Paul applies the image to Christ in 1 Corinthians 15:25 ('until he has put all his enemies under his feet'; cf. Psalms 110:1).
25: ʾal-tîrāʾû wĕʾal-tēḥāttû ḥizqû wĕʾimṣû — 'do not be afraid and do not be dismayed; be strong and courageous.' This formula appears throughout Joshua (Joshua 1:6,7,9; Joshua 10:25) and is explicitly God's word to Joshua in Joshua 1 — here Joshua passes it to his commanders. kî-kāzōt yaʿăśeh YHWH lĕkol-ʾōyĕbêkem — 'for this is what YHWH will do to all your enemies' — the military lesson is theological: the subjugated kings are a pledge of future victories, enacted by YHWH.
26: wayyakkēm yĕhôšuaʿ ʾaḥărê-kēn wayyĕmîtēm wayyitlēm ʿal ḥāmēšāh ʿēṣîm — 'Joshua struck them, killed them, and hanged them on five trees.' Hanging on trees/poles was post-mortem humiliation (Deuteronomy 21:22-23) — it declared the executed person under God's curse (qilĕlat ʾĕlōhîm tālûy, Deuteronomy 21:23). Paul quotes this in Galatians 3:13: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law, having become a curse for us — for it is written, cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree.'
27: At sunset the bodies are removed and placed in the cave — the same cave that had been their hiding place. wayyaggillû ʾăbānîm gĕdōlôt ʾal-pî hamĕʿārāh — the stones seal the cave permanently; 'they are there to this day' (ʿad-hayyôm hazzeh) is an etiological formula marking a visible landmark. The cave becomes both grave and memorial.
28: The southern mopping-up campaign begins with Makkedah. kĕkōl ʾašer ʿāśāh lĕmelek yĕrîḥô — 'as he did to the king of Jericho.' The Jericho formula becomes the standard comparison for complete destruction (ḥērem); each city's destruction is measured against the paradigmatic first conquest. The refrain structures Joshua 10:28-39.
29: Israel moves to Libnah. The formulaic repetition ('then Joshua passed and all Israel with him') tracks the campaign's rapid tempo. Libnah will later figure in Isaiah's narrative of Sennacherib's campaign (Isaiah 37:8).
30: Libnah destroyed — king, inhabitants, all. The formula lōʾ hiśʾîr bāh śārîd ('he left no survivor') appears throughout the southern campaign. The LXX occasionally varies this formula; the MT is consistent. The refrain emphasizes totality, not body-count precision.
31: Lachish — strategically the most significant Shephelah fortress. Three days of siege (Joshua 10:32) versus one-day victories elsewhere suggests genuine military resistance. Lachish's later prominence (Jeremiah 34:7; 2 Kings 14:19; the Lachish Letters) confirms its regional importance. Horam of Gezer comes to aid Lachish and is destroyed — relief forces are as futile as the besieged garrisons.
32: wayyitten YHWH ʾet-lākîš bĕyad yiśrāʾēl — 'YHWH gave Lachish into Israel's hand.' The divine passive appears at each major victory; the narrative refuses to attribute conquest to military skill alone. The two-day timeline for Lachish (compared to one-day or all-at-once for others) may indicate a more formidable defense requiring full siege tactics.
33: Horam king of Gezer comes in relief. wayyak ʾōtô yĕhôšuaʿ wĕʾet-ʿammô ʿad-bilti hišʾîr-lô śārîd — 'Joshua struck him and his people until none remained.' The formula absorbs the relief force into the same annihilation refrain. Gezer itself will not be fully conquered (Joshua 16:10; 1 Kings 9:16) — an anticipation of the incomplete conquest acknowledged in Joshua 13.
34: Eglon taken in one day. The speed contrasts with Lachish's two-day siege; different cities present different levels of resistance. The campaign maintains momentum — no pauses for consolidation that might allow regroupment.
35: The Eglon formula: king, every person in it, all (kol-nepeš). Nepeš (soul/person/life) is used as 'every living person' in destruction formulas — it is not a theological claim about eternal souls but the standard Hebrew idiom for 'every inhabitant.'
36: Hebron — city of Abraham (Genesis 13:18; Joshua 23:2), later David's first capital (2 Samuel 2:1). Its capture here is temporary; it reverts (Joshua 14:12-15; Joshua 15:13-14) as Caleb's allotted city. The formulaic destruction here and the later re-engagement with Hebron illustrate how the conquest summary in Joshua 10-11 is idealized: total victory is asserted even as subsequent chapters show it was partial.
37: Hebron: king, all its cities, all persons. The mention of 'all its cities' (kol-ʿārêhā) signals Hebron's regional control. The pattern of total destruction followed by resettlement (and eventual Canaanite persistence) is a recurring tension in the book.
38: Debir — mentioned in the conquest summary and later in Caleb's daughter's request (Joshua 15:15-17; Judges 1:11-13). The repeated conquest tradition (here in Joshua 10; again in Joshua 15 via Othniel) reflects either variant traditions or a reconquest after Canaanite resettlement.
39: The Debir formula completes the southern sweep. kĕkōl ʾašer ʿāśāh lĕḥebrôn — 'as he had done to Hebron, so he did to Debir.' The southern campaign is complete; each city measured by the same standard of total destruction.
40: wayyak yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-kol-hāʾāreṣ hāhār wĕhannegeb wĕhašŝĕpēlāh wĕhāʾăšēdôt — 'Joshua struck all the land: the hill country, the Negev, the western foothills, and the slopes.' The geographic summary covers the complete southern zone. kāʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'as YHWH God of Israel commanded' — the obedience formula anchors the destruction in divine mandate, not military ambition.
41: From Kadesh-barnea (the boundary of the wilderness) to Gaza (the coast) and from Goshen (not the Egyptian Goshen but a Negev region) to Gibeon — this is the full south-to-north extent of the southern campaign. The geographic markers frame the victory as comprehensive.
42: wĕʾet-kol-hamĕlākîm hāʾēlleh wĕʾet-ʾarṣām lāqaḥ yĕhôšuaʿ paʿam ʾeḥāt — 'all these kings and their land Joshua took at one time.' Paʿam ʾeḥāt ('one time/one stroke') is emphatic — the rapidity of the campaign is a theological point: YHWH's hand moved swiftly. kî YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl nilḥam lĕyiśrāʾēl — 'because YHWH the God of Israel fought for Israel.' The campaign's summary is theological before it is military.
43: wayyāšob yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkol-yiśrāʾēl ʿimmô ʾel-hammaḥăneh haggillgālāh — 'Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.' The return to base closes the southern campaign. Gilgal was Israel's cultic center and base of operations; the return marks the formal end of each campaign phase before the next begins (Joshua 11's northern campaign).
